description:
A deployment engineer wants a very quick point-in-time CPU-bound micro-benchmark before and after pushing a new software build.  
    Your goal is to gather the current baseline numbers and store them in a single, machine-readable log so that a future run of the same procedure can be compared with today’s results.

    1.  Create the directory  
        /home/user/benchmark  
        if it does not already exist.

    2.  Measure how long it takes to calculate a SHA-256 checksum for each of the following three, always-present binaries.  
        (We choose these tiny but common files so the measurements finish quickly under 1 s each in most containers.)  
            • /usr/bin/ls  
            • /usr/bin/find  
            • /usr/bin/grep  

        Use the POSIX-compliant builtin timing utility in “portable” mode:

            time -p <command>

        The -p flag ensures the timing summary is emitted in the **exact** three-line format

            real <seconds>
            user <seconds>
            sys  <seconds>

        where “seconds” is a decimal number (e.g. 0.27).

    3.  Extract the three timing numbers (real, user, sys) for each command and append them to a CSV file named  

            /home/user/benchmark/sha256_timing.csv

        The CSV **must** follow these rules so the automated test can parse it correctly:

            • The very first line is the header  
                  command,real,user,sys

            • Exactly three additional lines follow – one for each command – and they must appear in **this order**:

                  sha256sum /usr/bin/ls,<real>,<user>,<sys>
                  sha256sum /usr/bin/find,<real>,<user>,<sys>
                  sha256sum /usr/bin/grep,<real>,<user>,<sys>

            • “<real>”, “<user>”, and “<sys>” are the numbers taken directly from the time output  
              (do **not** include any extra spaces, units, or text).

            • Use a newline (ASCII 0x0A) to separate every line; do **not** add extra blank lines.

    4.  Make sure the file permissions allow the current non-root user to read the results later (e.g. mode 0644 is fine).

    When you are done, the tester will inspect /home/user/benchmark/sha256_timing.csv and verify:

        • The file exists.
        • The header matches exactly: command,real,user,sys
        • There are exactly 4 lines in total.
        • The three data lines appear in the prescribed order.
        • Each timing field is a valid (integer or floating-point) number.

    Do not print the timing numbers in your chat response; they only need to be inside the CSV file.
truth:
Expected file: /home/user/benchmark/sha256_timing.csv

    Line-by-line template (the automated checker will accept any valid decimal number in place of <float>):

        1. command,real,user,sys
        2. sha256sum /usr/bin/ls,<float>,<float>,<float>
        3. sha256sum /usr/bin/find,<float>,<float>,<float>
        4. sha256sum /usr/bin/grep,<float>,<float>,<float>

    <float> must match the regular expression: ^[0-9]+(\.[0-9]+)?$

    The file must contain **exactly** 4 lines (no trailing blank lines) and be world-readable (permissions 0644 or stricter).
